perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed a nd enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed a nd enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New York to Delaware without any specific justification and without the consent of both parties could be seen as an attempt to circumvent New York law.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract involving at least $250,000 to specify New York law as governing, regardless of where the transaction takes place. By attempting to change the jurisdiction to Delaware, the alteration creates uncertainty. Since CVS Pharmacy Inc. is a Rhode Island corporation, and the Vendor's location is not specified, the law could easily apply, and it would be difficult to make the case to change the jurisdiction without consent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The change from New York to Delaware as the governing law potentially contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401. This law, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows parties to a contract of $250,000 or more to agree that New York law governs, regardless of whether the contract has a reasonable relation to the state. The Law Explanation suggests the original contract could fall under this law. By changing the jurisdiction to Delaware without clear consent or justification, the modification undermines the enforceability and intent of the original choice of law clause, creating a structural flaw that could lead to legal disputes, particularly if New York law was intentionally chosen under § 5-1401. The change represents a move away from a potentially legally protected position under New York law.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor is strictly prohibited from placing any CVS Information on portable computing/storage devices, which are not owned and secured by Vendor. Vendor will take all reasonable, necessary, and appropriate measures, including encryption, to ensure that CVS Information stored on Vendor owned and secured devices cannot be accessed by una uthorized/inappropriate individuals.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor is strictly prohibited from placing any CVS Information on portable computing/storage devices, which are not owned and secured by Vendor. Vendor will take measures, to ensure that CVS Information stored on Vendor owned and secured devices cannot be accessed by una uthorized/inappropriate individuals.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'reasonable, necessary, and appropriate' qualifier weakens the obligation on the Vendor. The language implies they will take action to secure the data but removes the legal standard by which those actions will be judged.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law 201 CMR 17.03(1) requires businesses to implement and maintain a comprehensive information security program, including 'reasonable security measures'. Removing 'reasonable' weakens the standard of care, potentially contradicting the requirement for reasonable security.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'reasonable, necessary, and appropriate' qualifier, which weakens the vendor's obligation to secure CVS information. The law explanation states that Massachusetts law 201 CMR 17.03(1) requires businesses to implement 'reasonable security measures'. By removing 'reasonable', the modified contract potentially contradicts the legal requirement for reasonable security. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 201 CMR 17.00, which establishes minimum standards for safeguarding personal information, further emphasizing the importance of reasonable security measures as part of those standards.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor shall give CVS notice immediately if CVS Information has been accessed by unauthorized individuals or if there is any evidence of any such infraction.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor shall give CVS notice within 30 days if CVS Information has been accessed by unauthorized individuals or if there is any evidence of any such infraction.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediately' to 'within 30 days' significantly delays the notification period, impacting CVS's ability to respond to a data breach promptly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Various state data breach notification laws (e.g., California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA))
perturbation.law_explanation: While the contract does not state explicitly that California law is what is being used, many state data breach notification laws, including the CCPA, require notification 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay'. A 30-day delay is likely to be considered unreasonable, therefore in contradiction of the consumer rights that are established in the CCPA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'immediately' to 'within 30 days' introduces a clear contradiction with data breach notification laws. The Law Explanation states that state data breach notification laws require notification 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay'. The 30-day delay would likely be considered unreasonable. The CCPA snippet describes consumer rights, and the delay impacts CVS's ability to respond to a data breach promptly.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and agrees that money damage s would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the other party s hall be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or forestall any such br each or threatened breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and agrees that money damage s would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the other party may be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or forestall any such br each or threatened breach.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'may' makes injunctive relief discretionary rather than mandatory, weakening the ability of a party to prevent or stop a breach of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General contract law principles regarding specific performance and remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 states that specific performance (similar to injunctive relief) may be granted when monetary damages are inadequate. By making injunctive relief discretionary, this clause weakens the enforceability of the agreement, contradicting the principle that equitable remedies are available when legal remedies are insufficient.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' weakens the enforceability of injunctive relief. The original text mandates the availability of injunctive relief, while the changed text makes it discretionary. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 'Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract...'. By making injunctive relief discretionary, the changed text contradicts the principle that specific performance (similar to injunctive relief) should be available when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by a breach.
